markdown:
<definition term="Visualization">The process of **representing data or information graphically** to make it easier to understand, analyze, and interpret</definition>

1. It transforms **raw data** into **visual formats** such as charts, graphs, maps, and diagrams.
2. This allows users to **identify patterns, trends, and insights** that might be difficult to discern from text-based data alone.

<callout type="example">A line graph showing the **trend of sales over time** is a simple visualization that helps businesses track performance and make informed decisions.</callout>

# Two-Dimensional Visualization


<definition term="Two-Dimensional Visualisation">The **representation of data or information** in a **flat (two-dimensional) space**, such as a **graph**, **chart**, or **image**.</definition>

<callout type="note">Two-dimensional visualization is a **powerful tool** for **communicating** **complex** **data** in a **simple**, **visually** **appealing** **format**.</callout>

## Examples of 2D-Visualization


### Scatter Plots


1. Scatter plots are used to **visualize** the **relationship** between **two** **variables**.
2. Each point on the plot represents a **pair** of values, with the **x-axis** representing one variable and the **y-axis** representing the other.

<callout type="example">A scatter plot showing the relationship between **hours** **studied** and **test** **scores** for a group of students.</callout>

### Heat Maps


1. Heat maps use **color** to represent the **values** of a **matrix** or **grid** of data.
2. Each cell in the grid is colored based on its value, with **darker** colors representing **higher** values and **lighter** colors representing **lower** values.

<callout type="example">A heat map showing the **average** **temperature** in different regions of a country.</callout>

### Bar Charts


1. Bar charts are used to **compare** the **values** of **different** **categories**.
2. Each bar represents a category, with the **height** of the bar corresponding to its value.

<callout type="example">A bar chart showing the **population** of different countries.</callout>

### Pie Charts


1. Pie charts are used to **show** the **proportion** of **different** **categories** in a **whole**.
2. Each slice of the pie represents a category, with the **size** of the slice corresponding to its proportion.

<callout type="example">A pie chart showing the **market** **share** of different smartphone brands.</callout>
